finding: Temporal-policy calibration should use redacted metrics derived only from independently verified OIDC claims. Persisting raw JWTs or identity-bearing claim values is unnecessary and expands disclosure risk.

primary_sources.supports: GitHub Actions OIDC tokens expose exp, iat, jti, nbf and workflow/repository/run identity claims.
exp, nbf, and iat are NumericDate claims; JWTs may contain privacy-sensitive information and omission minimizes privacy risk.
All cryptographic operations must validate and issuer, subject, and audience require application validation.

design_implication: Insert a privacy-safe observation step after cryptographic, issuer, and audience validation. Retain only bounded timing deltas and claim-presence booleans; never retain the JWT, signature, or identity-bearing values in calibration evidence.

verification.method: Executed before commit from byte-identical source and test files.
covered: redacted output excludes issuer, audience, subject, jti, workflow SHA, repository ID, and run ID
issuer or audience validation absence rejected
malformed or excessive temporal profiles rejected
observations too distant from issuance rejected

uncertainty: Synthetic tests establish the redaction and boundary contract. They do not establish GitHub's observed token lifetime distribution; production observations are still required before changing the R-007 defaults.
falsification_route: Show that any raw identity-bearing claim value appears in serialized output.
Show that unverified, issuer-unvalidated, or audience-unvalidated claims can produce an observation.
Show that out-of-bound temporal values can enter calibration evidence.
Collect verified production observations demonstrating that the current bounds do not cover legitimate GitHub-issued tokens.
privacy_boundary: No live token, signature, credential, repository identity, workflow identity, user identity, private chat, health data, or arbitrary claim value was stored. Test fixtures are synthetic.
trust_limit: A valid observation is calibration evidence only. It does not authorize deployment, prove replay uniqueness, establish token authenticity beyond its upstream verification assertion, or prove scientific or deployment claims.
